content.problemStatement:
You are given an array AAA consisting of NNN non-negative integers.

It is guaranteed that AAA contains **at least two distinct** elements.

You are also given a non-negative integer XXX.

For each YYY (0≤Y≤X)(0 \leq Y \leq X)(0≤Y≤X), we create an array BBB of size NNN such that Bi=Ai∣YB_i = A_i | YBi​=Ai​∣Y, where ∣|∣ denotes the bitwise OR operation.

Find the **largest** integer Y(Y≤X)Y(Y \leq X)Y(Y≤X) such that the array BBB contains **at least two distinct** elements.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains two integers NNN and XXX — the length of the array AAA and maximum value of YYY allowed.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1,A_2,\ldots,A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​,  representing the array AAA.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, the largest YYY satisfying the condition.
constraints: The sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains two integers N and X — the size of the array and the maximum allowed value of Y. The second line contains N space‑separated non‑negative integers A1, A2, …, AN. It is guaranteed that the array contains at least two distinct elements and 0 ≤ Ai, X < 2^30.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing the largest integer Y (0 ≤ Y ≤ X) such that after replacing every Ai by Ai | Y the resulting array contains at least two distinct values.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: Y=5 gives B=[5,5,7] – two distinct values, and no larger Y ≤5 works.
Test case 2: Y=10 gives B=[14,15,10,11,15] – distinct values exist, and 10 is the maximum allowed.
Test case 3: Y=1 gives B=[1,1,3] – distinct, while Y=2 makes all elements 3, so answer is 1.

solution.approach: Let D be the bitmask of all positions where the original array is not uniform, i.e. D = OR_{i}(Ai xor A0). After applying Y, all elements become equal iff Y contains all bits of D ((Y & D) == D). Therefore we need the maximum Y ≤ X that does NOT satisfy (Y & D) == D. We build the answer greedily from the most significant bit to the least: for each bit, try to set it in the current answer, keep the candidate ≤ X, and ensure that the candidate still misses at least one bit of D (or equivalently that the array after OR with the candidate still has at least two distinct values). If both conditions hold we keep the bit, otherwise we leave it 0. This yields the largest feasible Y. The check can be performed directly by verifying that the array after OR with the candidate has at least two distinct values (function ok). The algorithm runs in O(N·B) per test case, where B = 30 (constant), which easily fits the limits.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N·30) per test case, i.e., O(N) overall because the number of bits is constant.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) to store the input array.
